zotero.abstractNote: Time pressure is a ubiquitous constraint in decision-making, yet its effect on the exploration-exploitation trade-off remains poorly understood. We investigate how time pressure influences human exploration in a multi-armed bandit task. Using a computational model of human behavior, we show that time pressure leads to a decrease in directed exploration (uncertainty-directed search) while leaving undirected exploration (random search) largely unaffected. This shift is driven by a reduction in the cognitive resources allocated to planning, as time pressure forces individuals to rely on simpler, more heuristic-based strategies. Our results provide a mechanistic account of how time pressure alters the exploration-exploitation trade-off and suggest that the impact of time pressure on decision-making is mediated by the cognitive costs of exploration.
